# 2026-07-13 13:40:49Z

<!-- Claude Code Session 5c7d5c23-0691-4467-b98e-0fb865b4a639 (2026-07-13 13:40:49Z) -->

_**User (2026-07-13 13:40:49Z)**_

In the repo's GitHub remote web UI interface, I just saw that several PRs have piled up. Some are by external users, we'll ignore those here. I just want to focus on the automatic regular PRs generated by the project's CI/CD on weekly list updates every Thursday, the ones titled "Best-of update: YYYY.MM.DD". In the web UI, I just merged PR "Best-of update: 2026.06.25", URL https://github.com/JuDFTteam/best-of-atomistic-machine-learning/pull/567. Problem now, and this is a recurring problem, whenever several of those automatic PRs have piled up, is that when I merge the oldest or an older one of them, the subsequent ones are not mergable anymore without merge concflict resolution. I guess this is due to GitHub or git misunderstanding the way this repo works, namely, that the README.md is auto-generated from the projects.yaml. I see that also latest-changes.md has a conflict. What I want for you to do is to analyze the open auto-generated PRs (568, 572) and tell me why this auto-merge failure occurs in this scenario, and how we could fix it -- individually and in general, and programmatically from your side and manually from my side, if I choose to do the latter once. Because this is annoying. In the past, I had resolved this by first closing the older piled up auto-generated PRs and then only merging the latest one. This is okay, I guess, we don't lose much info that way. But when I forget to do that and merge an older auto-generated PR first, then we are stuck now in this merge conflict situation and need to resolve it.

---

_**Agent (claude-opus-4-8 2026-07-13 13:43:02Z)**_

Verified the mechanics end-to-end. Here's the analysis.

## Why it happens

Your intuition is right, and it's narrower than "git misunderstands the repo." The key facts I confirmed:

| Fact | Evidence |
|------|----------|
| Auto-PRs **never touch [projects.yaml](projects.yaml)** (the real source of truth) | `git diff main...update/2026.07.02 -- projects.yaml` → empty |
| They only **regenerate [README.md](README.md) + [latest-changes.md](latest-changes.md)** and **add** dated files under [history/](history/) | PR file lists: both are `MODIFIED`; history files are `ADDED` |
| The conflict is **only** in README.md and latest-changes.md | `git merge-tree main update/2026.07.02` → `CONFLICT (content)` in exactly those two, history files merge clean |
| PR 568 branched from **before** 567 merged | merge-base = `ac4dc93` (the commit before your 567 merge `358d9c7`) |

The mechanism: README.md and latest-changes.md are **generated artifacts that are committed to git**. Every weekly run rewrites the *same* large blocks of these files (rankings, stats, trending lists) from live stats. When you merge one auto-PR, main's copy of those two files changes. A pending auto-PR was generated off an *older* main and rewrote the same lines. Git sees "both sides changed the same lines since the common ancestor" → textual conflict. It has no way to know README.md is derivable from projects.yaml, so it can't just regenerate.

The `history/YYYY-MM-DD_*.csv` and `_changes.md` files never conflict — each run adds uniquely-named files.

**Important corollary:** since projects.yaml is untouched, an older auto-PR's README is *stale the moment a newer auto-PR exists*. Merging 568 before 572 gains you almost nothing except 568's dated history snapshot — which is exactly why your "close the old ones, merge the newest" workaround loses so little.

## Fix now — 568 and 572

Your past workaround is still the correct lazy move: **close 568, merge only 572.** But 572 will *also* conflict against current main (it too branched pre-567). Two clean ways:

**Option A — you, manually, once (recommended, ~1 min).** Close 568 in the web UI, then resolve 572 locally by taking the regenerated files wholesale (there's nothing to hand-merge in generated files):

```bash
gh pr close 568 --delete-branch --comment "Superseded by 572."
git checkout main && git pull
git merge origin/update/2026.07.09
git checkout --theirs README.md latest-changes.md   # take the fresh regeneration
git add README.md latest-changes.md                  # history/ files auto-merge clean
git commit --no-edit && git push
```
`--theirs` = the PR branch = the newest regeneration. Any tiny drift is corrected by next Thursday's run anyway.

**Option B — cleanest, ignores the branches entirely.** Close both 568 and 572 and just re-run the workflow (Actions → update-best-of-list → Run workflow). It branches fresh from current main → no conflict, one clean PR. You lose the 07.02 and 07.09 history snapshots.

I can run Option A for you right now if you want.

## General fix — stop the pile-up

Root cause of the *recurring* pain isn't the conflict, it's the **pile-up**: multiple long-lived auto-PRs branched off stale mains. Kill the pile-up and the conflict can't form. Laziest robust fix: have the workflow **close any existing open auto-update PRs before creating the new one**, so exactly one auto-PR ever exists. That's your manual workaround, automated — and it matches the fact that older ones are always superseded.

One step added to [.github/workflows/update-best-of-list.yml](.github/workflows/update-best-of-list.yml), right after the first checkout (line 28), before `create-update-branch`:

```yaml
      - name: close-stale-update-prs
        shell: bash
        env:
          GITHUB_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }}
        run: |
          # Older auto-update PRs are always superseded by this run (README.md /
          # latest-changes.md are fully regenerated), so close them to prevent the
          # pile-up that causes merge conflicts when you merge them out of order.
          gh pr list --state open --json number,headRefName \
            --jq '.[] | select(.headRefName | startswith("${{ env.BRANCH_PREFIX }}")) | .number' \
          | while read -r pr; do
              gh pr close "$pr" --delete-branch --comment "Superseded by a newer best-of update." || true
            done
```

This runs before this run's branch/PR exists, so it only closes prior ones. Result: every Thursday you get exactly one PR, branched from current main, that can't conflict. You keep the manual merge click.

Skipped: `.gitattributes` union/`theirs` merge drivers (union-merging README = garbage output), and auto-merging the PR (removes your review click — say the word if you'd rather it self-merge and I'll swap this step for `gh pr merge --auto`).

Want me to (a) apply Option A to clear 568/572 now, (b) add the workflow step, or both?

---

_**User (2026-07-13 13:46:28Z)**_

<tool-use data-tool-type="write" data-tool-name="Write"><details>
<summary>Tool use: **Write**</summary>

```md
# Plan: Clear stuck auto-update PRs + prevent recurrence

## Context

The repo's weekly CI ("Best-of update: YYYY.MM.DD") opens PRs that **regenerate**
`README.md` and `latest-changes.md` and **add** dated files under `history/`. These
generated files are committed to git. When several such PRs pile up and one is merged,
the pending ones — branched off an older `main` — have rewritten the same lines in
`README.md`/`latest-changes.md`, so git reports a content conflict. Verified facts:

- Auto-PRs never touch `projects.yaml` (the real source of truth):
  `git diff main...update/2026.07.02 -- projects.yaml` → empty.
- `git merge-tree main update/2026.07.02` → `CONFLICT (content)` in **only**
  `README.md` and `latest-changes.md`; `history/` files merge clean (unique names).
- PR 568 branched from `ac4dc93` (before the 567 merge `358d9c7`).
- Because `projects.yaml` is untouched, an older auto-PR's README is stale the moment a
  newer auto-PR exists → older auto-PRs are always superseded.

Two goals: (A) unstick the current pile-up (568, 572) now; (B) stop it recurring.

## Part A — Clear the current pile-up (one-time, run from local clone)

568 is superseded by 572, so close it and merge only 572 with a take-theirs resolution
(generated files — nothing to hand-merge).

\```bash
cd /Users/wasmer/src/github.com/judftteam/best-of-atomistic-machine-learning
gh pr close 568 --delete-branch --comment "Superseded by 572."
git checkout main && git pull
git merge origin/update/2026.07.09
git checkout --theirs README.md latest-changes.md   # take the fresh regeneration
git add README.md latest-changes.md                  # history/ files auto-merge clean
git commit --no-edit
git push
\```

Notes:
- `--theirs` during `git merge` = the PR branch (update/2026.07.09) = newest regeneration.
- Merging via local push will close PR 572 automatically once `main` contains its commits.
- Any minor stat drift is corrected by the next Thursday run.

## Part B — Prevent recurrence (workflow edit)

File: `.github/workflows/update-best-of-list.yml`

Add one step that closes existing open auto-update PRs before this run creates its own,
guaranteeing exactly one auto-PR exists at a time (pile-up impossible → no conflict).
Keeps the manual merge click.

Insert **after** the first `- uses: actions/checkout@v4` (line 28) and **before**
`- name: create-update-branch` (line 34):

\```yaml
      - name: close-stale-update-prs
        shell: bash
        env:
          GITHUB_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }}
        run: |
          # Older auto-update PRs are always superseded by this run (README.md /
          # latest-changes.md are fully regenerated), so close them to prevent the
          # pile-up that causes merge conflicts when merged out of order.
          gh pr list --state open --json number,headRefName \
            --jq '.[] | select(.headRefName | startswith("${{ env.BRANCH_PREFIX }}")) | .number' \
          | while read -r pr; do
              gh pr close "$pr" --delete-branch --comment "Superseded by a newer best-of update." || true
            done
\```

Placement rationale: runs before this run's branch/PR exists, so it only closes prior
ones. `startswith(BRANCH_PREFIX)` ("update/") scopes it to auto-PRs, never human PRs.

The workflow change lands via a normal PR/commit to `main` (branch first — currently on `main`).

## Not doing (rejected alternatives)
- `.gitattributes` union merge driver → union-merged README is garbage.
- Auto-merge (`gh pr merge --auto`) → removes the review click the user wants to keep.

## Verification
- Part A: after push, `gh pr view 572 --json state` shows `MERGED`; 568 shows `CLOSED`;
  `git log --oneline -3` on `main` shows the merge; `README.md` reflects 07.09 stats.
- Part B: `actionlint .github/workflows/update-best-of-list.yml` (if available) or YAML
  parse; real validation is the next scheduled run — confirm it closes any leftover
  open `update/*` PR before opening the new one. Manual dry check: run the `gh pr list ...`
  pipeline locally to confirm it selects only `update/*` PRs.

```

## Approved Plan (edited by user):
# Plan: Clear stuck auto-update PRs + prevent recurrence

## Context

The repo's weekly CI ("Best-of update: YYYY.MM.DD") opens PRs that **regenerate**
`README.md` and `latest-changes.md` and **add** dated files under `history/`. These
generated files are committed to git. When several such PRs pile up and one is merged,
the pending ones — branched off an older `main` — have rewritten the same lines in
`README.md`/`latest-changes.md`, so git reports a content conflict. Verified facts:

- Auto-PRs never touch `projects.yaml` (the real source of truth):
  `git diff main...update/2026.07.02 -- projects.yaml` → empty.
- `git merge-tree main update/2026.07.02` → `CONFLICT (content)` in **only**
  `README.md` and `latest-changes.md`; `history/` files merge clean (unique names).
- PR 568 branched from `ac4dc93` (before the 567 merge `358d9c7`).
- Because `projects.yaml` is untouched, an older auto-PR's README is stale the moment a
  newer auto-PR exists → older auto-PRs are always superseded.

Two goals: (A) unstick the current pile-up (568, 572) now; (B) stop it recurring.

## Part A — Clear the current pile-up (one-time, run from local clone)

568 is superseded by 572, so close it and merge only 572 with a take-theirs resolution
(generated files — nothing to hand-merge).

```bash
cd /Users/wasmer/src/github.com/judftteam/best-of-atomistic-machine-learning
gh pr close 568 --delete-branch --comment "Superseded by 572."
git checkout main && git pull
git merge origin/update/2026.07.09
git checkout --theirs README.md latest-changes.md   # take the fresh regeneration
git add README.md latest-changes.md                  # history/ files auto-merge clean
git commit --no-edit
git push
```

Notes:
- `--theirs` during `git merge` = the PR branch (update/2026.07.09) = newest regeneration.
- Merging via local push will close PR 572 automatically once `main` contains its commits.
- Any minor stat drift is corrected by the next Thursday run.

## Part B — Prevent recurrence (workflow edit)

File: `.github/workflows/update-best-of-list.yml`

Add one step that closes existing open auto-update PRs before this run creates its own,
guaranteeing exactly one auto-PR exists at a time (pile-up impossible → no conflict).
Keeps the manual merge click.

Insert **after** the first `- uses: actions/checkout@v4` (line 28) and **before**
`- name: create-update-branch` (line 34):

```yaml
      - name: close-stale-update-prs
        shell: bash
        env:
          GITHUB_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }}
        run: |
          # Older auto-update PRs are always superseded by this run (README.md /
          # latest-changes.md are fully regenerated), so close them to prevent the
          # pile-up that causes merge conflicts when merged out of order.
          gh pr list --state open --json number,headRefName \
            --jq '.[] | select(.headRefName | startswith("${{ env.BRANCH_PREFIX }}")) | .number' \
          | while read -r pr; do
              gh pr close "$pr" --delete-branch --comment "Superseded by a newer best-of update." || true
            done
```

Placement rationale: runs before this run's branch/PR exists, so it only closes prior
ones. `startswith(BRANCH_PREFIX)` ("update/") scopes it to auto-PRs, never human PRs.

The workflow change lands via a normal PR/commit to `main` (branch first — currently on `main`).

## Not doing (rejected alternatives)
- `.gitattributes` union merge driver → union-merged README is garbage.
- Auto-merge (`gh pr merge --auto`) → removes the review click the user wants to keep.

## Verification
- Part A: after push, `gh pr view 572 --json state` shows `MERGED`; 568 shows `CLOSED`;
  `git log --oneline -3` on `main` shows the merge; `README.md` reflects 07.09 stats.
- Part B: `actionlint .github/workflows/update-best-of-list.yml` (if available) or YAML
  parse; real validation is the next scheduled run — confirm it closes any leftover
  open `update/*` PR before opening the new one. Manual dry check: run the `gh pr list ...`
  pipeline locally to confirm it selects only `update/*` PRs.
```
</details></tool-use>

cd /Users/wasmer/src/github.com/judftteam/best-of-atomistic-machine-learning
git add .github/workflows/update-best-of-list.yml
git commit -m "$(cat <<'EOF'
ci: close stale auto-update PRs before creating a new one

Weekly auto-update PRs regenerate README.md and latest-changes.md. When
several pile up and are merged out of order, the pending ones conflict on
those regenerated files. Since projects.yaml is untouched, older auto-PRs
are always superseded — so close any open update/* PRs before this run
opens its own, guaranteeing only one auto-PR exists at a time.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.8 <noreply@anthropic.com>
EOF
)" 2>&1
git push -u origin ci/close-stale-update-prs 2>&1
```

<tool-use data-tool-type="shell" data-tool-name="Bash"><details>
<summary>Tool use: **Bash**</summary>
Open PR for workflow change

```bash
cd /Users/wasmer/src/github.com/judftteam/best-of-atomistic-machine-learning
gh pr create --base main --head ci/close-stale-update-prs \
  --title "ci: close stale auto-update PRs before creating a new one" \
  --body "$(cat <<'EOF'
## Problem

The weekly `update-best-of-list` workflow opens a PR that **regenerates** `README.md` and `latest-changes.md` (and adds dated `history/` files). These generated files are committed. When several such PRs pile up and are merged out of order, the pending ones — branched off an older `main` — have rewritten the same lines and hit merge conflicts in `README.md`/`latest-changes.md`. `projects.yaml` (the real source) is never touched by these runs, so an older auto-PR is always superseded by a newer one.

## Fix

Add a `close-stale-update-prs` step that closes any open `update/*` PRs before this run creates its own. Guarantees exactly one auto-update PR exists at a time, so the pile-up (and the conflicts) can't happen. The manual merge click is unchanged.

Scoped to `update/` (BRANCH_PREFIX) branches only — never touches human PRs. Runs before this run's branch/PR exists, so it only closes prior ones.

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)
EOF
)" 2>&1
```

```text
https://github.com/JuDFTteam/best-of-atomistic-machine-learning/pull/573
```
</details></tool-use>

_**Agent (claude-opus-4-8 2026-07-13 13:53:34Z)**_

<tool-use data-tool-type="shell" data-tool-name="Bash"><details>
<summary>Tool use: **Bash**</summary>
Delete all stale remote update branches

```bash
cd /Users/wasmer/src/github.com/judftteam/best-of-atomistic-machine-learning
git branch -r | sed 's/ *origin\///' | grep '^update/' > /tmp/stale_update_branches.txt
echo "count: $(wc -l < /tmp/stale_update_branches.txt)"
# Delete in chunks of 50 refs per push
xargs -n 50 git push origin --delete < /tmp/stale_update_branches.txt 2>&1 | tail -20
```

_**Agent (claude-opus-4-8 2026-07-13 13:53:45Z)**_

_**Agent (claude-opus-4-8 2026-07-13 13:53:53Z)**_

Done.

- **PR #573 merged** into `main`; the `close-stale-update-prs` step is now live on `main`.
- **213 stale `update/*` remote branches deleted** (2023-08 → 2026-06); none existed locally; local refs pruned. None remain.

Tags and commit history are untouched — the workflow tags every version (e.g. `2026.06.25`), and all merges live in `main`, so deleting the branches loses nothing.

## How to do the branch cleanup yourself in the terminal

The one-liner I used, deleting all remote `update/*` branches in chunks:

```bash
git branch -r | sed 's| *origin/||' | grep '^update/' \
  | xargs -n 50 git push origin --delete
```

- `git branch -r` lists remote-tracking branches; `sed` strips the `origin/` prefix; `grep '^update/'` keeps only the auto-update ones.
- `xargs -n 50` batches 50 refs per `git push --delete` so the command line doesn't get too long (`git push origin --delete a b c ...` deletes multiple at once).

Then sync your local view (remote-tracking refs linger until pruned):

```bash
git fetch --prune origin
```

Local `update/*` branches, if you ever have any:

```bash
git branch | grep 'update/' | xargs git branch -D
```

Safety check before deleting — preview first by dropping the `xargs` pipe (`... | grep '^update/'` alone just prints the list). And `gh pr list --state open --search "head:update/"` confirms none still have an open PR before you nuke them.
